criteria: The agent has read Section 3 (Method) completely to understand the CompAct methodology, including the forward pass activation compression, backward pass compressed gradient computation, and Adam optimizer update with low-rank moments.

The agent has read Algorithm 1 to understand that during the forward pass a Gaussian random projection matrix P is sampled using a per-layer seed, the compressed activation z = xP ∈ R^{b·l×r} is saved for the backward pass (not x), and the full output o = xW_t is computed and returned.

The agent has read Algorithm 2 to understand that during the backward pass the compressed weight gradient Ĝ_t = z^T · (∂L/∂o) ∈ R^{r×m} is computed, while the input gradient ∂L/∂x = (∂L/∂o) · W_t^T ∈ R^{b·l×n} is computed in full rank.

The agent has read Algorithm 3 to understand that Adam first and second moment estimates M_t and V_t are maintained in the compressed subspace R^{r×m}, and the decompressed gradient is G̃_t = α·P·N_t where P is resampled with the same seed as the forward pass.

The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand that the projection matrix P is a Gaussian random matrix with μ=0 and σ=1/r, sampled independently per layer, and that using the same random seed in both forward and backward passes eliminates the need to store P in memory.

The agent has read Section 4.1 and Appendix B to understand the pretraining setup: LLaMA-based models (60M, 130M, 350M, 1B) trained on C4, sequence length 256, batch size 512, cosine LR scheduler with 10% warmup to 0.1 of initial LR, scaling factor α=0.25, and projection update period T=50 for 60M–350M and T=200 for 1B.

The agent has read Section 4.2 and Appendix C to understand the finetuning setup: RoBERTa-Base finetuned on all 8 GLUE tasks, 30 epochs, batch size 16 (32 for CoLA), max sequence length 512, α=2 for r=8 and α=4 for r=4, T=500, and results averaged over 3 seeds.

The agent has read Appendix B.2 to understand that CompAct is applied to all linear modules except the attention output-projection layer (due to shared FlashAttention memory), and that for 60M–350M models the output-projection gradient is scaled by a separate α_out = 0.5α.

The plan includes implementing a custom linear layer (or autograd Function / hook) that computes and saves the compressed activation z = xP during the forward pass instead of x, while still computing and returning the full output o = xW_t.

The plan specifies that the random projection matrix P will be sampled on-the-fly from N(0, 1/r) using a per-layer integer seed, and that the same seed will be reused during the backward pass and Adam update to resample the identical P without storing it.
The plan includes modifying the Adam optimizer so that first and second moment estimates M_t and V_t are stored and updated in the compressed subspace R^{r×m} rather than R^{n×m}.

The plan specifies implementing the decompression step G̃_t = α·P·N_t, where P is resampled using the same per-layer seed as the forward pass to project optimizer output back to the full weight space.
The plan specifies that r is defined as a fraction of each layer's input dimensionality n (e.g., r = n/2, n/4, n/8) rather than as a single fixed integer shared across all layers.
The plan includes applying CompAct to all attention and MLP linear layers (Q, K, V, gate, up, down projections) but explicitly excluding the attention output-projection layer.
The plan includes running pretraining experiments on LLaMA-60M, 130M, 350M, and 1B with Full-Rank, GaLore, and CompAct at r=n/2, n/4, n/8, measuring perplexity and peak GPU memory.
The plan includes finetuning RoBERTa-Base on all 8 GLUE tasks (CoLA, STS-B, MRPC, RTE, SST2, MNLI, QNLI, QQP) with CompAct r=4 and r=8, compared against Full Fine-Tuning and GaLore r=4 and r=8.

The plan includes ablation studies for: (a) projection matrix type comparison on LLaMA-60M, (b) projection update period T on LLaMA-130M, (c) rank vs. training steps on LLaMA-130M, and (d) batch size effect on peak GPU memory for LLaMA-350M.

A custom linear forward function is implemented such that the compressed activation z = xP ∈ R^{b·l×r} is computed and saved for the backward pass instead of the full input x, while the full output o = xW_t ∈ R^{b·l×m} is still computed and returned.

The random projection matrix P is sampled from N(0, 1/r) (variance 1/r, not 1) independently per layer using a per-layer seed, and the seed-based resampling mechanism allows P to be identically reproduced during the backward pass and Adam update step without ever storing P in memory.
The backward pass correctly computes the compressed weight gradient Ĝ_t = z^T · (∂L/∂o) ∈ R^{r×m} using the saved compressed activation z rather than the full input x.

The backward pass correctly computes the full input gradient ∂L/∂x = (∂L/∂o) · W_t^T ∈ R^{b·l×n} using the original uncompressed weight matrix, ensuring the backward signal through the network is unaffected by the compression.

The Adam optimizer maintains first and second moment estimates M_t ∈ R^{r×m} and V_t ∈ R^{r×m} (initialized to zero) in the compressed subspace, updated as M_t = β1·M_{t-1} + (1-β1)·Ĝ_t and V_t = β2·V_{t-1} + (1-β2)·Ĝ_t^2.
The Adam update step includes bias correction, dividing M_t by (1-β1^t) and V_t by (1-β2^t), before computing the normalized update N_t = M_t / (√V_t + ε).

The decompression step G̃_t = α·P·N_t is implemented using the same per-layer seed as the forward pass to resample an identical P ∈ R^{n×r}, projecting the low-rank optimizer output back to the full weight space R^{n×m} for the weight update.

The projection update period T is correctly implemented: the per-layer seed is incremented by 1 every T optimizer steps (i.e., when step t mod T == 0), causing the projection matrix P to rotate to a new random matrix at that frequency.

The rank r is computed as a fraction of each layer's input dimensionality n (e.g., r = n // 2 for ratio 1/2, r = n // 4 for ratio 1/4, r = n // 8 for ratio 1/8), not as a single fixed integer applied uniformly across layers.

CompAct is applied to all linear layers in the attention mechanism (Q, K, V projections) and MLP blocks (gate, up, down projections) of each Transformer layer, but the attention output-projection layer is explicitly excluded and trained without activation compression.

For pretraining LLaMA-60M to 350M, the attention output-projection layer's gradient scale is set to α_out = 0.5α (half of the main scaling factor) to mitigate instability caused by inconsistent effective learning rates across layers with and without compression.

The pretraining implementation uses a LLaMA-based architecture with RMSNorm and SwiGLU activations, trains on the C4 dataset without data repetition, with maximum sequence length 256 and batch size 512.

The pretraining uses a cosine learning rate scheduler that decays to 0.1 of the initial learning rate, with a warmup period for the first 10% of training steps, and a global scaling factor α=0.25 applied to all CompAct weight updates.

Training steps per model size match Table 5: 10K steps for 60M, 20K for 130M, 60K for 350M, and 100K for 1B, with the optimal learning rate selected per model size from the range [1e-3, 1e-2], and projection update period T=50 for 60M–350M and T=200 for 1B.

The finetuning setup correctly configures RoBERTa-Base on all 8 GLUE tasks for 30 epochs with batch size 16 (32 for CoLA), maximum sequence length 512, T=500, α=2 for r=8 and α=4 for r=4, and reports mean performance over 3 random seeds.

Finetuning evaluation metrics follow the GLUE conventions: F1 score for QQP and MRPC, Matthews Correlation for CoLA, Pearson Correlation for STS-B, and classification accuracy for SST2, MNLI, QNLI, and RTE.

For the projection matrix ablation study, all four projection types are implemented: (1) independent Gaussian per layer, (2) shared-seed Gaussian across layers, (3) Sparse Johnson-Lindenstrauss matrix, and (4) VeLoRA projection matrix.

For the batch size ablation on LLaMA-350M, training is run without gradient accumulation across batch sizes 16, 32, 64, 128, and 256, measuring peak GPU memory for CompAct (r=n/4), GaLore (rank=256), and the full-rank baseline.
Pretraining experiments are successfully executed without errors for all four LLaMA model sizes (60M, 130M, 350M, 1B) under all conditions: Full-Rank, GaLore, and CompAct with r=n/2, r=n/4, r=n/8.
Finetuning experiments are successfully executed without errors for CompAct (r=4, r=8) and GaLore (r=4, r=8) on all 8 GLUE tasks using RoBERTa-Base.
Throughput and peak memory comparison experiments are successfully executed for LLaMA-350M with all configurations: CompAct r=n/2, n/4, n/8, GaLore, CompAct r=n/8 + CKPT, GaLore + CKPT, and baseline.
Ablation experiments on projection matrix types (Gaussian, shared-seed Gaussian, Sparse JL, VeLoRA) are successfully executed on LLaMA-60M with r=n/4 using learning rates from {1e-2, 5e-3, 1e-3}.
Ablation experiments on projection update period T are successfully executed on LLaMA-130M across multiple T values (1, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 5K, 10K, 20K) for rank ratios 0.5 and 0.25.
Ablation experiments on rank and training steps are successfully executed on LLaMA-130M with T=200 for ranks 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9 at 20K, 30K, and 40K training steps.

Batch size ablation experiments are successfully executed on LLaMA-350M for batch sizes 16, 32, 64, 128, and 256 without gradient accumulation, measuring peak GPU memory for CompAct, GaLore, and baseline.

Pretraining results in Table 2 are reproduced: CompAct r=n/8 achieves a peak GPU memory of approximately 33.03 GB for LLaMA-350M (vs. baseline 39.97 GB), a ~17% reduction, while maintaining perplexity under 22; for LLaMA-1B, CompAct r=n/8 fits within ~81 GB where baseline does not.

Memory breakdown results in Table 3 are reproduced for LLaMA-350M at r=n/4: activations reduced from ~7.0 GB to ~2.87 GB, gradients from ~0.65 GB to ~0.26 GB, optimizer states from ~1.3 GB to ~0.52 GB, and peak memory from ~39.97 GB to ~34.71 GB.

Finetuning results in Table 4 are reproduced: CompAct r=4 achieves approximately 50% peak memory reduction (~3092 MB vs. Full Fine-Tuning ~6298 MB) with an average GLUE score around 85.03, and CompAct r=8 around 85.56, both comparable to GaLore.

Throughput results in Figure 3(a) are reproduced: CompAct r=n/8 achieves approximately 47.30 tokens/sec (above GaLore at ~44.51), and CompAct r=n/8 + CKPT achieves ~35.50 tokens/sec vs. GaLore + CKPT at ~34.50 tokens/sec.
Peak device memory results in Figure 3(b) are reproduced: CompAct r=n/8 uses ~33 GB vs. GaLore ~39.25 GB vs. baseline ~39.97 GB; with CKPT, CompAct uses ~8.90 GB vs. GaLore CKPT ~9.60 GB.
Projection matrix ablation results in Figure 4 are reproduced: Gaussian (CompAct) achieves perplexity ~34.41, JL ~34.80, shared-seed Gaussian ~36.06, and VeLoRA ~66.86 on LLaMA-60M with r=n/4.

Projection update period T ablation results in Figure 5 are reproduced for LLaMA-130M: an optimal T range emerges around 50–500 for both rank 0.5 and 0.25, with very low T (e.g., T=1) or very high T (e.g., T=20K) both leading to worse perplexity.

Rank vs. training steps ablation results in Figure 6 are reproduced for LLaMA-130M: at 40K steps, rank 0.5 achieves perplexity ~23.5 and rank 0.25 achieves ~24.8, with lower ranks requiring more training steps to approach the performance of higher ranks.

Batch size ablation results in Figure 7 are reproduced: at batch size 256, CompAct (r=n/4) uses ~68 GB vs. GaLore ~75 GB vs. baseline ~76 GB; at small batch sizes (≤32), all three methods converge to similar memory usage, confirming that CompAct's benefit scales with batch size.
